stories.headline: CISA Adds Critical Splunk Enterprise Flaw to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
stories.summary: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added a significant flaw in Splunk Enterprise, tracked as CVE-2026-20253, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. Agencies are urgently urged to patch this vulnerability by Sunday to prevent potential exploitation in critical infrastructure.
stories.original_title: [Securityaffairs.com] U.S. CISA adds Splunk Enterprise flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and urges agencies to fix it by Sunday — U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) adds Splunk Enterprise flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) added a Splunk Enterprise flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-20253…
stories.context: Imagine your computer has a digital lock, and hackers have found a way to pick it without anyone noticing. Splunk is a popular software program that companies use to watch their computer systems for trouble, like a security camera for data. CISA is the U.S. government agency responsible for protecting the country’s essential services, such as power grids and hospitals, from cyberattacks. They added this specific flaw to a "known exploited" list, which is essentially a public warning sign telling all organizations that hackers are actively using this hole right now, so they must fix it immediately before they get broken into.
stories.why_it_matters: Hackers are actively using a known weakness in popular software to break into computer systems. Fixing this bug quickly is essential to stop criminals from stealing data or shutting down important services like power grids and hospitals.
stories.other_side: Some IT teams argue that forcing an immediate patch might cause other systems to crash if they aren't tested first, potentially creating new problems while trying to fix old ones.

stories.headline: WWDC 2026: Apple Doubles Down on AI with Siri and Expanded Intelligence
stories.summary: Apple's WWDC 2026 was dominated by artificial intelligence announcements, introducing a new Siri AI and significantly expanding Apple Intelligence capabilities across iPhone, iPad, and Mac. The keynote highlighted how AI is reshaping user interaction and device functionality, marking a strategic pivot for the tech giant.
stories.original_title:
[Macworld] Apple’s silence about HomePod and Apple TV at WWDC spoke volumes — Macworld



WWDC 2026 was almost entirely focused on AI announcements. Apple introduced Siri AI, expanded the capabilities of Apple Intelligence across the iPhone, iPad, and Mac, and spent a significant amount of time discussing how AI enables new ways f…
stories.context: Apple has always been known for making phones that are easy to use but sometimes lack the newest features compared to competitors. Recently, artificial intelligence—computer programs that can think and learn like humans—became the most important thing in the tech world. At WWDC, which is Apple’s annual conference for developers, they announced major updates to Siri and other tools to make them smarter and more helpful. This shift shows that Apple is trying hard to catch up with rivals like Google and Microsoft by putting AI at the center of everything you do on your devices.
stories.why_it_matters: Apple is making artificial intelligence a central part of its devices, which means your phone and computer will soon do more tasks for you automatically. This changes how people interact with technology, making devices smarter but also more dependent on cloud processing.
stories.other_side: Critics worry that relying more on AI means Apple collects even more of your personal data, raising serious concerns about privacy and how that information is used.

stories.headline: Deep Learning Market Projected to Surge to Nearly $2 Trillion by 2035
stories.summary: A new report by SNS Insider projects the global deep learning market to reach $1,963.25 billion by 2035, driven by massive investments in generative AI and enterprise automation. The U.S. market alone is expected to grow from $37.14 billion in 2025 to nearly $600 billion, signaling a transformative era for AI infrastructure.
stories.original_title: [GlobeNewswire] Deep Learning Market to Reach $1,963.25 Billion by 2035 as Generative AI and Enterprise Automation Drive Massive AI Investments | Research by SNS Insider — The U.S. Deep Learning Market is Projected to Grow from $37.14 Billion in 2025 to $596.02 Billion by 2035, While Europe is Expected to Expand from $31.98 Billion to $451.64 Billion, Fueled by Rapid Generative AI Adoption, Large-Scale AI Infrastructure Investm…
stories.context: Deep learning is a type of artificial intelligence where computers learn from massive amounts of information, similar to how a child learns to recognize animals by looking at many pictures. For years, this technology was mostly used in research labs or by big tech companies. Now, businesses everywhere are realizing that using these smart computers can save money and time by automating boring tasks. Because so many companies are investing billions of dollars into building the infrastructure needed for this technology, experts predict the market will grow to nearly two trillion dollars in the next decade.
stories.why_it_matters: The industry is pouring massive amounts of money into AI technology, expecting it to become a huge part of the global economy in the next decade. This growth will likely create many new jobs but also disrupt traditional industries as automation takes over more work.
stories.other_side: Skeptics point out that building this infrastructure requires enormous amounts of energy and rare materials, which could harm the environment and strain local resources.

stories.headline: UN Report Warns Digital Surveillance Threatens Democracy and Human Rights
stories.summary: A United Nations study has concluded that pervasive digital surveillance creates chilling effects that undermine democratic norms and political participation. The report identifies systemic threats to interconnected human rights posed by modern digital monitoring technologies.
stories.original_title: [ComputerWeekly.com] Chilling effects of surveillance threaten democracy, UN finds — United Nations study finds the chilling effects of pervasive digital surveillance in modern life undermines an entire web of interconnected and interdependent human rights, representing a systemic threat to democratic norms and political participation
stories.context: In a democracy, people need to feel free to speak their minds and vote without fear of being watched or punished. Digital surveillance refers to the use of cameras, internet tracking, and phone monitoring by governments or corporations to collect information about citizens. A recent United Nations report warns that when people know they are being watched, they become afraid to speak up or join protests, which hurts the health of a free society. The report argues that this constant watching threatens basic human rights and makes it harder for democracy to function properly.
stories.why_it_matters: The United Nations warns that constant digital monitoring can scare people into staying silent about political issues, which weakens democracy. If citizens feel watched, they may stop participating in public life or criticizing leaders.
stories.other_side: Governments argue that surveillance is necessary to keep the public safe from terrorism and crime, claiming that security outweighs minor privacy inconveniences.

stories.headline: Fisk University Integrates $400 Million Data Center into Campus Revitalization
stories.summary: Fisk University has included a $400 million data center at the core of its $1 billion master plan to revitalize its Nashville campus. This move positions the historic HBCU as a hub for big tech infrastructure, blending educational revitalization with modern technological demands.
stories.original_title: [Forbes] Why Fisk University’s $1 Billion Master Plan Includes A Data Center — While some communities fight big tech, this 160-year-old HBCU has put a $400 million data center at the financial heart of its bid to revitalize its Nashville campus.
stories.context: Fisk University is a historic college in Nashville that was founded after the Civil War specifically to educate African Americans, known as an HBCU (Historically Black College and University). Like many older schools, it has faced financial challenges and needs money to repair its old buildings and improve its campus. To solve this, they are partnering with a tech company to build a huge data center on their land, which generates steady income by storing internet data for other companies. This plan allows the university to pay for its own revitalization while becoming part of the modern technology industry.
stories.why_it_matters: A historic university is using a massive data center project to fund its own renewal, showing how tech infrastructure can support education. This model could help other schools pay for upgrades by hosting the servers that power the internet.
stories.other_side: Local residents worry that the data center will use too much water for cooling and increase traffic, negatively impacting the quiet campus neighborhood.

stories.headline: DevOps Leaders Warn Against Faking Value Delivery in Agile Transformations
stories.summary: New insights from DevOps.com highlight a common organizational failure where high deployment frequency does not translate to actual value delivery. Experts argue that many companies misunderstand the fundamentals of Agile and DevOps, leading to bottlenecks where strategy still takes months to reach customers despite green dashboards.
stories.original_title: [DevOps.com] You Cannot Fake Flow: What Organizations Get Wrong About Value Delivery — Most organizations that have invested heavily in Agile and DevOps share a puzzling experience. Deployment frequency is up. Teams are busy. Dashboards are green. And yet value still queues. Strategy still takes months to reach the customer. Feedback still arri…
stories.context: DevOps is a way of working where computer programmers and IT workers team up to release software updates faster and more often. Many companies started doing this because they thought releasing new features quickly meant they were successful. However, experts warn that just pushing out code fast does not help customers if the new features are not actually useful or if the underlying strategy is flawed. It is like running very fast in the wrong direction; you might be moving quickly, but you are not getting any closer to solving the real problem for your users.
stories.why_it_matters: Companies are realizing that just releasing software updates faster does not mean they are actually improving their products. Teams need to focus on solving real customer problems rather than just chasing speed metrics.
stories.other_side: Some managers believe that high-speed releases are still valuable because they allow companies to test ideas quickly and fail fast without wasting too much money.

stories.headline: Tech Giant Acquires Space Tech Firm MDA Space Buys Blue Canyon Technologies
stories.summary: MDA Space has announced a definitive agreement to acquire US-based Blue Canyon Technologies LLC, expanding its total addressable market and strengthening its position in the US defense sector. The acquisition adds a profitable business with an 18-year history to MDA's portfolio, expected to be accretive to earnings.
stories.original_title: [PRNewswire] MDA Space announces definitive agreement to acquire US-based Blue Canyon Technologies LLC — Expands Total Addressable Market for MDA Space Positions company to further pursue substantial US defence market opportunities Adds a profitable, cash-generating business with 18-year history Transaction expected to be accretive to Adjusted EBITDA1 and Adjust…
stories.context: MDA Space is a Canadian company that builds satellites and space equipment, while Blue Canyon Technologies is an American company that makes small parts for satellites, like batteries and navigation tools. By buying Blue Canyon, MDA is not just getting a new product line; they are gaining a strong foothold in the United States, which is a huge market for defense and space contracts. This kind of business move is common in the space industry, where companies merge to combine their skills and win more government jobs that require both design and manufacturing capabilities.
stories.why_it_matters: A major space company is buying a smaller US firm to strengthen its hold on defense contracts, consolidating power in the aerospace sector. This trend suggests that smaller innovators may be absorbed by larger players rather than staying independent.
stories.other_side: Smaller competitors argue that these big mergers reduce innovation because large corporations tend to be slower and less creative than agile startups.

stories.headline: Review: TECNO POVA Curve 2 5G Offers High Refresh Rate and Battery Life
stories.summary: YugaTech reviews the TECNO POVA Curve 2 5G, noting that while large batteries and high refresh rate displays are now common, this device stands out in its segment. The review evaluates whether the phone successfully balances these key tech specs for consumers seeking performance without premium pricing.
stories.original_title:
[Yugatech.com] TECNO POVA Curve 2 5G Review — These days, it’s not hard to find a smartphone with a large battery or a high refresh rate display. Finding...
The post TECNO POVA Curve 2 5G Review appeared first on YugaTech | Philippines Tech News & Reviews.
stories.context: When buying a smartphone, two important features are how smooth the screen looks when you scroll (called refresh rate) and how long the battery lasts between charges. The TECNO POVA Curve 2 is a budget-friendly phone that includes these high-end features, which were once only found in expensive models. Reviewers are checking if this phone delivers good performance for its low price, especially since having a large battery and a smooth screen usually costs more money. It represents a trend where cheaper phones are starting to offer the same speed and endurance as premium brands.
stories.why_it_matters: New budget phones are finally offering features like long battery life and smooth screens that were once only found in expensive models. This makes better technology accessible to more people who cannot afford premium brands.
stories.other_side: Reviewers note that cheaper phones often have lower-quality cameras and slower processors, meaning users still get a compromised experience compared to top-tier devices.

stories.headline: Reader Switches from Kindle to DRM-Free E-Reader for Better Control
stories.summary: An Android Authority contributor details their decision to abandon Amazon Kindles due to restrictive DRM policies affecting older devices. They switched to the Xteink X4, citing the need for ownership rights and freedom from platform lock-in as primary drivers for adopting DRM-free e-readers.
stories.original_title: [Android Authority] I gave up on Kindles for this DRM-free e-reader, and I’m never going back — Amazon won't let you download e-books to older Kindles. I don't want that to happen to me, so I bought an Xteink X4.
stories.context: DRM stands for Digital Rights Management, which is a technical lock that prevents you from copying or moving digital files, like e-books, to different devices. Amazon’s Kindle uses this system, meaning if you buy a book, you are technically just renting it from Amazon and cannot easily read it on a different brand of reader. Some readers are frustrated by this because they feel they should own the books they buy, so they are switching to brands like Xteink that allow files to be moved freely. This shift highlights a growing desire among consumers to have true ownership of their digital purchases rather than being locked into one company’s ecosystem.
stories.why_it_matters: Readers are rejecting e-readers that lock them into one brand, preferring devices where they truly own their books. This shift pressures companies to offer more freedom, allowing users to switch platforms easily.
stories.other_side: Big retailers argue that strict controls are needed to prevent piracy and ensure authors get paid fairly for every copy sold.

stories.headline: Schindler Elevators Integrate Dynamic Lighting and Media for Architectural Tech
stories.summary: Schindler is redefining transit technology by integrating dynamic lighting and media into elevator systems to create immersive architectural experiences. This approach combines material honesty with advanced tech features to transform mundane transit seconds into cohesive journeys.
stories.original_title:
[Designboom] schindler elevates the seconds of transit into a cohesive architectural journey — schindler engineers elevators that transform the daily transit into an immersive experience through material honesty, dynamic lighting, and integrated media.
The post schindler elevates the seconds of transit into a cohesive architectural journey appeared fir…
stories.context: Elevators are usually seen as purely functional machines designed only to move people up and down buildings quickly. Schindler, a major elevator manufacturer, is changing this by treating elevators as part of the building’s art and design. They are adding screens and dynamic lighting inside the elevator cars to create interesting visual experiences while people wait or ride. This approach turns a boring few seconds of transit into an engaging moment, showing how technology can be used to enhance human experience even in small, everyday spaces.
stories.why_it_matters: Elevators are becoming interactive spaces with lights and screens, turning boring wait times into engaging experiences. This shows how everyday infrastructure can be upgraded to improve how people feel during daily commutes.
stories.other_side: Maintenance workers worry that adding complex electronics to elevators will make repairs harder and more expensive when things inevitably break down.
